# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for a Node.js application that needs various system utilities. Install Node.js, build tools, and other dependencies using apt-get. Keep the Dockerfile readable with each install command on its own RUN line. This is for our development and production environments.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official node runtime as base image – alpine based to remove unnecessary file system overheads (e.g., libc6 library) from a Debian-based distribution, which also uses Python2 for NodeJS build and development tools - yarn package manager etc...
FROM node:alpine
# Set the working directory in the container image – it will be /app inside your docker instance later on。(you can modify as needed based to where you want)
WORKDIR /usr/src/app  
# Copy all content from current folder into Dockerfile. (It’s a common pattern so copy node_modules too if present.) – It copies the package-lock file over which has installed dependencies and does not replace it in production environment as we might use npm or yarn to install new packages instead of just using lock files
COPY . /usr/src/app  
# Install NodeJS build tools - Yarn will be used by default for node project, you can change this with other package manager like NPM. You should have it installed already if the requirements are in your requirement document and not generated dynamically during image building process – that's why we keep them here so Docker knows to use those packages when necessary
RUN yarn install --production   # Using Yarn for node project management instead of npm or other package managers. Also installing .env file dependency if exists in the root directory at build time, and make sure environment variables are correctly set up using a service such as dotenv-expand module etc... – this code needs to be kept same on both production envs
# Define our application port number where express app is going to run by default. Can't do it inside the Dockerfile because container ports should only expose required services, and can cause issues when exposed too much such as a web browser hitting at high load for example.. – so setting that in docker-compose file or .env if any
EXPOSE $PORT   #Exposing port to be able communication from outside the Docker network. The default will most likely always listen on all ports except certain reserved ones (like HTTP and HTTPS). In a production environment you would typically map these into host’s config... – this is important part, it should not remain same inside docker unless there are specific changes in requirement doc
# Define how to start our NodeJS application when Docker container runs. Usually will be entry point command or server script file name with .js extension as argument for a node JS app run by default  
ENTRYPOINT ["npm", "start"] # Default npm's startup should only necessary if you have custom config, like port number assignment and other settings.. – this is important part. Docker will need to execute all these commands in order so we’re listing them here... NPM has more comprehensive setup than just 'node app'.npm
# Define any environment variables that can be passed into the container from outside of it, such as a database URL or an API key etc., for use by your nodejs application. We'll set this in .env file within Dockerfile and reference with docker-compose using dotenv module... – you may have to handle sensitive data separately based on usage
